title: AUTOSAR Classic Platform
subtitle: 10 stages · 30–60 minutes/stage · Architecture × System Design × BSW × OS × RTE
description: Advanced learning for AUTOSAR Classic architecture, system design, stack configuration, generation, system services, OS, RTE and ISO 26262 interfacing.
methodTitle: Reason as an architect, not as a tool operator
methodSteps: Start from the vehicle or ECU intent.
Explain the AUTOSAR model and responsible modules.
Close with timing, safety, diagnostics and verification consequences.

caseStudy.title: ESC / Brake-Control ECU
caseStudy.description: One domain case is traced from SWC and system design through RTE, OS, communication, system services, MCAL, configuration, generation and functional-safety evidence.

notice: Release-neutral, vendor-neutral learning material. Verify module details against the AUTOSAR release and vendor stack used by the target program. Current public Classic release at creation time: R25-11.
focusNote: Connect vehicle behavior to SWC and system models, ECU allocation, generated RTE/BSW realization, execution timing, diagnostics, safety mechanisms and verification evidence. Avoid stopping at module definitions or tool-click sequences.

architectureExercise.title: 15-minute architecture exercise
architectureExercise.prompt: Design and defend the AUTOSAR Classic architecture for a brake-by-wire ECU with networked wheel-speed sensors and a remote hydraulic actuator.
structure: Clarify vehicle function, timing, safety and availability.
Define SWCs, ports, interfaces and ECU allocation.
Trace RTE, OS and communication realization.
Define SysStack, diagnostics, memory and startup behavior.
Explain configuration/generation workflow and supplier contracts.
Close with safety mechanisms, verification evidence and trade-offs.
challenges: Wheel-speed data becomes stale during a turn.
RTE execution exceeds the 1 ms budget.
A BSW vendor update changes generated APIs.
One post-build dataset is loaded into the wrong hardware variant.
A QM diagnostic task disturbs an ASIL-D control task.

days.title: AUTOSAR Architecture and Core Concepts
days.focus: Classic vs Adaptive, layered architecture, VFB, SWCs, RTE and BSW boundaries.
days.deliverable: Draw an ESC ECU layered architecture and explain one end-to-end data path.

days.title: System Design in AUTOSAR
days.focus: SWC decomposition, interfaces, data types, deployment, communication mapping and timing.
days.deliverable: Create a logical SWC design and port map for ESC or brake control.

days.title: Methodology, ARXML and Generation Workflow
days.focus: System Description, ECU Extract, ECUC, RTE phases, variants and multi-party handovers.
days.deliverable: Produce an artifact flow from vehicle model to generated ECU binary.

days.title: BSW Architecture, Configuration and Generation
days.focus: BSW layers, MCAL, ECU abstraction, services, CDD, initialization and generated integration.
days.deliverable: Build a BSW module and initialization dependency map.

days.title: Communication Stack in Depth
days.focus: COM, PduR, CanIf, CanDrv, state managers, transport protocol, timing and E2E.
days.deliverable: Trace and configure one safety-related CAN signal end to end.

days.title: SysStack and System Services in Depth
days.focus: EcuM, BswM, WdgM, Dem, Det, FiM, NvM, Dcm and coordinated ECU modes.
days.deliverable: Draw startup, shutdown and fault-mode interactions between system services.

days.title: AUTOSAR OS in Depth
days.focus: Tasks, events, scheduling, resources, timing services, ISR, protection and multicore.
days.deliverable: Create an OS task/runnable map with priorities and timing budgets.

days.title: RTE in Depth
days.focus: Contract, communication semantics, runnable events, concurrency, BSW services and debugging.
days.deliverable: Define RTE APIs and activation semantics for a brake-control SWC.

days.title: Integration, Variants and Troubleshooting
days.focus: Toolchain stages, configuration dependencies, build integration, diagnostics, multicore and performance.
days.deliverable: Create a configuration review and integration-debug checklist.

days.title: Advanced Architecture and Safety Integration
days.focus: End-to-end design, ISO 26262 interface, supplier integration, tool confidence and leadership scenarios.
days.deliverable: Present a 15-minute brake-by-wire AUTOSAR architecture and evidence strategy.

tracks.name: Architecture
tracks.desc: Classic Platform layers, VFB, BSW boundaries, CDD and platform selection.
tracks.iso: AUTOSAR Classic Platform architecture; interface to ISO 26262 system/software architecture

tracks.name: System Design
tracks.desc: SWCs, compositions, interfaces, deployment, communication design and timing.
tracks.iso: Software Component Template and System Template

tracks.name: Workflow & ARXML
tracks.desc: AUTOSAR methodology, model exchange, extracts, ownership and change control.

tracks.name: BSW & Configuration
tracks.desc: MCAL, ECU abstraction, services, ECUC, variants, initialization and generation.
tracks.iso: Classic Platform BSW and ECU Configuration

tracks.name: Communication Stack
tracks.desc: COM, PduR, CanIf, CanDrv, CanTp, CanSM, ComM, Nm and E2E.
tracks.iso: AUTOSAR communication services and hardware abstraction

tracks.name: SysStack
tracks.desc: EcuM, BswM, WdgM, Dem, Det, FiM, NvM, Dcm and ECU modes.
tracks.iso: AUTOSAR system, diagnostic and memory services

tracks.name: OS
tracks.desc: Tasks, events, priorities, resources, timing services, protection and multicore.

tracks.name: RTE
tracks.desc: Contract, communication APIs, events, concurrency, service access and generation.

tracks.name: Diagnostics & Memory
tracks.desc: Dcm, Dem, FiM, NvM and persistent/diagnostic integration.

tracks.name: Toolchain & Generation
tracks.desc: Validation, generation, code integration, build, variants and reproducibility.
tracks.iso: AUTOSAR Methodology, ECUC and ISO 26262-8 tool confidence interface

tracks.name: Multicore & Performance
tracks.desc: Core allocation, timing, memory, contention, RTE and BSW optimization.
tracks.iso: AUTOSAR OS/RTE multicore concepts and timing analysis

tracks.name: Safety Integration
tracks.desc: E2E, WdgM, OS protection, FFI, safety manuals and ISO 26262 mapping.

matrix: Vehicle control function
SWC decomposition, ports, modes, timing and actuator requests
SWC Template, VFB, RTE
Keep functional interfaces independent of ECU and transport while preserving safety/timing semantics.
E/E system design
ECU allocation, network topology and communication mapping
System Template and ECU Extract
Trace vehicle-level intent to each ECU and control cross-supplier ownership.
ECU software architecture
Application/RTE/BSW layering and module selection
Classic Platform architecture
Justify boundaries, CDD use, partitioning and non-functional trade-offs.
CAN communication
Signal-to-PDU-to-frame mapping and stack configuration
COM, PduR, CanIf, CanDrv, CanSM, ComM, Nm
Connect system design to ECUC and verify timing, modes and fault behavior end to end.
Execution and timing
Runnable activation, tasks, ISR, resources and budgets
RTE and AUTOSAR OS
Demonstrate response-time and data-age reasoning, not only task configuration.
ECU lifecycle
Startup, wakeup, communication modes, shutdown and reset
EcuM, BswM, ComM and state managers
Design deterministic mode transitions and failure recovery.
Diagnostics
Events, DTCs, UDS services and function inhibition
Dem, Dcm, FiM, CanTp
Separate safety reaction, diagnostic qualification and service access.
Persistent data
Integrity, defaults, read/write strategy and endurance
NvM, MemIf, Fee/Ea
Define validity and recovery behavior under interrupted or corrupted storage.
Functional safety
FFI, supervision, protected communication and safe modes
OS protection, WdgM, E2E, Dem and BSW/MCAL safety manuals
Map ISO 26262 requirements to mechanisms and evidence without claiming AUTOSAR compliance equals safety compliance.
Configuration and variants
Model ownership, ECUC, generation and post-build data
Methodology, ARXML and ECU Configuration
Ensure reproducible generation, compatibility and variant validation.
Supplier integration
ARXML contracts, stack releases, generators and assumptions
Methodology, BSWMD, ECU Extract, safety manual
Control interfaces, releases, responsibility and change impact across organizations.
Multicore ECU
Core mapping, shared resources, memory and interference
AUTOSAR OS/RTE multicore support
Analyze cross-core latency, contention and dependent failures.
focusMap: Layered architecture, component boundaries, platform selection and trade-offs
SWCs, interfaces, data types, ECU mapping and network design
ARXML, ECU Extract, ECUC, variants, generators and CI
COM/PduR/CanIf/CanDrv, state managers, CanTp and E2E
EcuM, BswM, WdgM, Dem, Det, FiM, NvM and Dcm
Scheduling, resources, timing, protection and multicore
Contract, APIs, events, concurrency and BSW service interaction
ISO 26262 allocation, FFI, safety mechanisms and supplier assumptions
Recurring domain examples connecting vehicle control to AUTOSAR
Initialization, communication, timing, generated-code and integration failures

quiz.q: Which artifact is normally the ECU-specific subset of system-level AUTOSAR information?
quiz.why: The ECU Extract carries system-description information relevant to one ECU and feeds ECU development/configuration.

quiz.q: Which module packs application signals into I-PDUs?
quiz.why: COM handles signal semantics and I-PDU packing/unpacking; PduR routes PDUs.

quiz.q: Which mechanism primarily supervises runnable/control-flow checkpoints?
quiz.why: WdgM provides alive, deadline and logical supervision using configured checkpoints.

quiz.q: What is the design-time abstraction that permits SWC communication before ECU allocation?
quiz.why: The Virtual Functional Bus models component communication independently of concrete ECU/network realization.

options: RTE is a dynamic operating system
A generated configuration still requires system-level verification
CDD is preferred for all fast control code
quiz.why: Generation confirms translation of a model, not correctness of requirements, timing, safety or integration behavior.
